items.correct: Usually not — full capacity leaves no slack for maintenance, breakdowns or new orders, and can strain staff and quality; most firms target a high but not full level.
items.misconception: Operations should always run at full capacity.
items.random_wrong: Utilisation above 95% is prohibited by safety law.
items.why: Students equate maximum utilisation with maximum efficiency, ignoring flexibility and quality costs.

items.q: Does higher labour productivity always mean lower total costs?
items.correct: Not automatically — if the gains are bought with big pay rises or expensive equipment, unit costs may not fall; compare output gains with the cost of achieving them.
items.random_wrong: Productivity is the number of products stored in the warehouse.
items.why: Students treat productivity gains as free; unit labour cost depends on pay as well as output.

items.q: Is holding buffer stock just wasted money?
items.correct: No — buffer stock ties up capital but protects against supply delays and demand surges; the right level balances holding costs against stock-out costs.
items.misconception: The best stock level for every business is zero.
items.random_wrong: Buffer stock is stock kept aside for staff parties.
items.why: After learning JIT, students over-apply it; resilience arguments cut the other way.

items.correct: The firm produces 90 percent of its capacity
items.misconception: The firm produces 10 percent of its capacity
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are 90 percent of total
items.why: It's The firm produces 90 percent of its capacity. “The firm produces 10 percent of its capacity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Lowers unit cost. “Raises unit cost” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 75. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
